destination.vibe: 80 miles north of Seattle at the foot of the North Cascades, where Chuckanut Drive clings to the cliffs above Samish Bay, a $2 county ferry puts you on a car-optional island 10 miles from downtown, and SR-542 climbs 4,100 feet through old-growth forest to the Coleman Glacier at Artist Point.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August (Tue/Wed departures cheapest; 5 nonstop flights/week from DTW)
flights.notes: DTW→SEA nonstop. July peak expect $380–470 RT. Bike fee: $30–75 each way depending on fare class and case dimensions — verify at alaskaair.com before booking. Getting SEA→Bellingham: (A) Transit — Link Light Rail SeaTac to King Street Station, Seattle (40 min, $3.25), then Amtrak Cascades to Fairhaven Station, Bellingham (~2h, $27–45; bikes accepted with $10 reservation — book early at amtrak.com or 1-800-USA-RAIL, July slots fill fast); (B) Car — rent at SEA ($55–80/day compact), drive I-5 north 80 miles, 90 minutes. A car is required for the Mt. Baker day; transit-only travelers should add a 1-day local rental in Bellingham.

transportation.name: Amtrak Cascades — King Street Station (Seattle) → Fairhaven Station (Bellingham)
transportation.description: Scenic rail along Puget Sound · 4 trains daily · bikes accepted
transportation.notes: Reserve bike space early at amtrak.com or 1-800-USA-RAIL — July Cascades fill fast. Fairhaven Station is 3 flat miles from downtown Bellingham.

transportation.name: Car Rental at SEA — I-5 north to Bellingham (80 mi, ~90 min)
transportation.description: Seattle-Tacoma Airport → Bellingham · required for the Mt. Baker day
transportation.notes: Budget, Enterprise, and Hertz all have desks at SEA. Reserve early — July inventory is tight. A compact hatchback handles a soft-sided bike bag.

lodging.name: Hotel Leo
lodging.highlights: 1920s boutique hotel with a confirmed locked bike room, dedicated bike wash station, a prohibition-era cocktail bar, and a private 30-seat screening room — more cycling infrastructure than most hotels twice the price.

lodging.name: Heliotrope Hotel
lodging.highlights: Renovated 1950s boutique property at 2419 Elm St with a dedicated bike washing station, seven distinct room layouts, a fire pit yard, and a pet-friendly vibe — 10 minutes from downtown by bike.

lodging.name: Chrysalis Inn & Spa
lodging.highlights: Waterfront hotel on Bellingham Bay with gas fireplaces in every room, on-site spa, hot tub, and Keenan's at the Pier restaurant — Pacific Northwest cuisine with direct views of the bay and San Juan Islands.

lodging.name: Tiny House — Mt. Baker View
lodging.highlights: Entire guesthouse on a 4-acre wooded property with Mt. Baker views — loft bed, fire pit, projector and sound bar, bamboo landscaping, and an off-grid composting outhouse with on-demand shower. The furthest-from-town option and the most memorable.

lodging.name: Walnut Hut — Farm Stay
lodging.neighborhood: Rural Bellingham (6 miles from downtown, near Lynden)
lodging.highlights: Rustic cabin on a 9-acre permaculture biodynamic farm — double bed, outdoor kitchen (April–October), seasonal farmstand, farm tours by appointment, shared bath in a nearby building. 17 miles from the Canadian border and a completely different pace than the city.

itinerary.overview: Bellingham is the kind of Pacific Northwest city cyclists move to on purpose. It sits 80 miles north of Seattle on Bellingham Bay, with the North Cascades rising immediately east, the San Juan Islands visible from the shoreline, and Chuckanut Drive — a 23-mile cliff-hugging two-lane above Samish Bay — beginning right at the city's southern edge. The 10-day structure works in three arcs: Bellingham as base (Chuckanut Drive, Lummi Island via a $2 county ferry, a rest day to explore the city) for the first four days; the San Juan Islands for three days of island-hopping from Lopez to Orcas to San Juan Island via Washington State Ferry; and Mt. Baker for the penultimate day — 24 miles and 4,100 feet of climbing up SR-542 to Artist Point at 5,140 feet, one of the best sustained road climbs in the country. July is Bellingham's best month: 65–75°F, low humidity, sunset after 9pm, and the Coleman Glacier in full view from the road's end.

days.title: Arrive — SEA → Bellingham, Fairhaven
days.morning: Fly DTW→SEA on Alaska Airlines (5h48m nonstop). From SeaTac: board Link Light Rail to King Street Station, Seattle (40 min, $3.25), then Amtrak Cascades north to Fairhaven Station, Bellingham (2h, $27–45, bike reservation required). OR pick up your rental car at SEA and drive I-5 north 80 miles (90 min). Fairhaven Station is beautifully placed — a covered platform in the historic village, 3 walkable blocks from the waterfront.
days.afternoon: Check in, assemble the bike. Stop at Kulshan Cycles (downtown) or Jack's Bicycle Center for any parts or mechanical needs — local shops, local road knowledge. Walk Fairhaven Village: Victorian-era brick storefronts, independent bookstores, the cobblestone main street. Village Books on 11th St is one of the great independent bookstores in the Pacific Northwest.
days.evening: Dinner at Dirty Dan Harris (named for Fairhaven's scandalous founding pioneer) or downtown. First beer at Aslan Brewing (1330 N Forest St, West Coast IPAs) or Wander Brewing (1807 Dean Ave) — both are stalwarts of the Bellingham craft beer scene.

days.title: Chuckanut Drive + Skagit Flats — 50mi / 80km, 2,418ft / 737m
days.morning: Roll out by 7:30am south through Fairhaven and immediately onto Chuckanut Drive (SR-11) — one of the first state highways in Washington, and still one of the most dramatic. The road clings to sandstone cliffs above Chuckanut Bay and Samish Bay for 23 miles, with views across to the San Juan Islands and, on clear mornings, Victoria, BC. No shoulder for much of it, moderate car traffic — ride with confidence and take the lane where needed. Stop at Larrabee State Park (mile 7) for the tidepools and bay view.
days.afternoon: Descend into Bow and turn east into the Skagit Valley farm loop — flat open roads through tulip and daffodil fields, with Mt. Baker's perfect cone rising above the farmland to the east. Lunch at Breadfarm in Bow-Edison: the best bread bakery in northwest Washington, housed in a converted barn, with sourdough and pastries worth stopping for. Return north to Bellingham via Chuckanut (retrace) or inland back roads — total loop approximately 50 miles.
days.evening: Recovery pint at Wander Brewing (1807 Dean Ave) or Kulshan Brewing (2238 James St). Dinner downtown — La Fiamma Wood Fire Pizza (200 E Chestnut St, the perennial local favorite) or Il Posto (Italian, Fairhaven, more ambitious menu).

days.title: Lummi Island via Whatcom Chief Ferry — 47mi / 76km
days.morning: Ride 10 miles south from Bellingham center on Haxton Way to Gooseberry Point — flat and direct, passing through Lummi tribal lands with bay views the entire way. The Whatcom Chief ferry departs Gooseberry Point every 30 minutes during daytime hours. This is a Whatcom County vessel, not a Washington State Ferry: walk-on with bike costs roughly $2–4 round trip. The crossing is 6 minutes.
days.afternoon: Circumnavigate the island: the perimeter road is 12 miles with rolling terrain and intermittent steep pitches — particularly Smugglers Cove Road on the south end. The north end is gentler with bay views toward the mainland. The island has no bike shops and very limited services — bring everything you need. Return ferry to Gooseberry Point and ride 10 miles back to Bellingham.
days.evening: Post-ride in Fairhaven or downtown. Wild Buffalo House of Music (larger stage, eclectic bookings) or check what's on at the Shakedown.

waypoints.name: Lummi Island south loop (Smugglers Cove Rd)

days.title: Rest Day — Whatcom Falls + Bellingham
days.morning: Whatcom Falls Park: a short ride or 10-minute drive from downtown. The falls pour over a basalt shelf into a canyon pool below a stone bridge — most dramatic in spring but beautiful year-round. Easy trails through the ravine, zero technical terrain.
days.afternoon: Whatcom Museum: three buildings downtown anchored by the 1892 Old City Hall, a massive red-brick Victorian on Prospect Street. The Lightcatcher Building (250 Flora St) has rotating contemporary exhibitions — free or suggested donation. Then rent a kayak on Bellingham Bay (several outfitters near Squalicum Harbor; protected water, good for any skill level).
days.evening: Downtown Bellingham's bar scene is better than a city of 92,000 has any right to expect. The Shakedown (1212 N State St, good cocktails, locally sourced menu) or get tomorrow's logistics sorted over coffee.

days.title: San Juan Islands, Day 1: Lopez Island — 37mi / 60km
days.morning: Drive to Anacortes ferry terminal (30 miles south, 40 minutes on Hwy 20 via I-5). Catch a mid-morning Washington State Ferry to Lopez Island (~1h30m; walk-on $15.85 each way, bike surcharge ~$2–4, no reservation needed for cyclists). Roll off the ferry at Upright Head and immediately feel the pace change — Lopez has the flattest terrain and the quietest roads of any island in the archipelago.
days.afternoon: Loop Lopez: 37–40 miles, gently rolling. Highlights: Shark Reef Sanctuary (lock the bike, 10-minute walk to rocky bluffs above a seal rookery with Olympic Mountain views), Spencer Spit State Park (spit extending into the water, good for a swim), Odlin County Park. Lunch at Fine Mess Bakery in Lopez Village — the island's social center, line out the door for good reason.
days.evening: Overnight on Lopez. MacKaye Harbor Inn (farmhouse B&B on the harbor, ~$175–200/night) or camping at Odlin County Park ($25–30). Check the WSF inter-island ferry schedule for your morning Lopez → Orcas crossing at wsdot.com/ferries.

days.title: San Juan Islands, Day 2: Lopez → Orcas, Mt. Constitution — 35mi / 56km, 1,900ft / 580m
days.morning: Catch the morning inter-island WSF ferry from Lopez to Orcas (~30–45 minutes, same walk-on fare). Disembark at Orcas Village. The ride from the ferry to Eastsound is 9 miles through Crow Valley — hilly immediately, narrow winding roads, a preview of Orcas's character. Stock up on food and water in Eastsound before heading into Moran State Park.
days.afternoon: Mt. Constitution: the definitive San Juan Islands climb. From the Moran State Park entrance (2 miles from Eastsound), the road winds 5 miles to the 2,409-foot summit with switchbacks reaching 15% grades. At the top: a CCC-built stone observation tower from 1936 and a view that on clear days spans from Mt. Baker to the Cascades to the Olympics to Vancouver Island. Descend carefully: the road is narrow and technical going down.
days.evening: Overnight on Orcas. Outlook Inn (171 Main St, Eastsound, ~$200–250/night) or camping at Moran State Park (reservations required in July at recreation.gov). Dinner in Eastsound at Mijita's (310 A St) or the New Leaf Café at Outlook Inn (171 Main St).

waypoints.name: New Leaf Café / Outlook Inn (171 Main St)

days.title: San Juan Islands, Day 3: Orcas → San Juan Island → Bellingham — 40mi / 64km
days.morning: Ferry from Orcas to Friday Harbor on San Juan Island (~1h30m, walk-on fare). Friday Harbor is the only incorporated town in the San Juan Islands — a working waterfront with coffee shops and a bike-friendly main street. Roll out immediately on the island loop: southwest along West Side Road to Lime Kiln Point State Park, the best land-based orca-watching site in North America. June through September, Southern Resident killer whales pass within yards of the rocky shore.
days.afternoon: Continue around the island: English Camp on the northwest shore (site of the British garrison during the 1859–1872 Pig War) and American Camp on the south end (the corresponding US outpost, now a windswept prairie with sweeping views). Total loop: 35–43 miles. Ferry from Friday Harbor back to Anacortes (~1h15m), then drive 40 minutes north to Bellingham.
days.evening: Back in Bellingham. Three islands, three ferries, 120 miles of island roads in three days. Rest.

waypoints.name: Lime Kiln Point State Park (whale watching)

days.title: Recovery — Nooksack Valley Farm Loop or Easy Spin
days.morning: Active recovery day: ride north through Whatcom County farm country toward the Nooksack River valley. Route options range from a flat 25-mile Lynden loop to a longer 45-mile agricultural circuit past berry farms and the Dutch-influenced main street of Lynden — the most pastoral riding in the county, essentially zero elevation gain.
days.afternoon: Return to Bellingham. Afternoon options: kayak on Squalicum Harbor, spa at Chrysalis Inn (804 10th St, Fairhaven — day passes available), or simply sleep horizontally with your legs elevated.
days.evening: Confirm tomorrow's Mt. Baker road status: call Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest road information at (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, or check nwroadinfo.org. Artist Point Road typically opens mid-July.

days.title: Mt. Baker SR-542 Hill Climb — 48mi / 77km roundtrip from Glacier, 4,100ft / 1,250m
days.morning: Drive 32 miles east from Bellingham on SR-542 to the town of Glacier, WA (elevation ~1,100ft, 50 minutes). Start climbing by 8am. SR-542 continues east 24 miles to Artist Point (5,140ft / 1,567m). The lower section follows the North Fork Nooksack River through massive old-growth Douglas fir — Nooksack Falls viewpoint at mile 10 (a roadside pull-off to a 170-foot waterfall, worth the 3-minute stop). The road then climbs past the Mt. Baker Ski Area, through heather meadows, to Artist Point below the permanent snowfield.
days.afternoon: Descend 24 miles back to Glacier — a long, technical alpine descent. Lunch at Graham's Store in Glacier (the only place to eat in town, solid burgers, the mountain atmosphere is earned). Drive back to Bellingham (50 min).
days.evening: Final evening in Bellingham. Wander Brewing or Aslan if the weather cooperates. Pack the bike tonight.

days.title: Depart — Bellingham → SEA
days.morning: Tony's Coffee Roastery (2033 Division St — Bellingham's coffee institution since 1971, the right send-off for any trip here). Fairhaven Station is a 3-mile ride from downtown Bellingham; Amtrak Cascades south to King Street Station, Seattle (2h, $27–45), then Link Light Rail south to SeaTac (40 min). OR drive the rental car back to SEA (90 min on I-5 south).

tips: Amtrak Cascades bike policy: space is limited and July fills fast — reserve your bike spot ($10 fee) well in advance at amtrak.com or 1-800-USA-RAIL. Fairhaven Station is 3 miles south of downtown Bellingham and entirely bikeable. Not all Cascades trains have a baggage car; confirm your specific train accepts bikes when booking.

Whatcom Chief ferry (Lummi Island): this is NOT a Washington State Ferry — it's a Whatcom County vessel. Total cost for a cyclist is roughly $2–4 round trip. Bikes are always welcome with no reservations possible or needed. Runs every 30 minutes 6am–10pm daily in summer. Gooseberry Point (the departure dock) is 10 flat miles south of downtown Bellingham on Haxton Way.

WSF San Juan Islands: walk-on passengers ($15.85 each way) never need a reservation — cyclists board last and almost always make the next sailing. Each inter-island segment (Lopez → Orcas, Orcas → Friday Harbor) carries its own fare. Download the summer schedule at wsdot.com/ferries before Day 5 and plan your island routing around departure times.

SR-542 / Artist Point road status: Artist Point Road (the final stretch past Mt. Baker Ski Area) typically opens mid-July as snowpack melts. Call Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest: (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, or check nwroadinfo.org. If Artist Point is still gated, summiting at the ski area is 22 miles / 3,700ft from Glacier — still a legitimate world-class climb.

July weather in Bellingham: the finest month of the year, averaging 65–75°F with the fewest rain days. Sunset at 9:10pm means early starts aren't required. The exception is Mt. Baker — it generates its own weather and can be 20–30°F colder at 5,000ft with clouds that don't exist in the valley. Pack a rain shell and arm warmers for Day 9 regardless of the valley forecast.

For the 7-day version: Day 1 arrive, Day 2 Chuckanut Drive, Day 3 Lummi Island, Day 4 San Juan Islands day trip to Lopez only (no overnight), Day 5 Orcas + San Juan Island in one long day (not recommended but possible), Day 6 Mt. Baker, Day 7 depart. The cleaner 7-day compress: skip Orcas and do just Lopez + San Juan Island as separate day trips from Anacortes on Days 4–5, then Mt. Baker on Day 6.

sources.title: Top biking experiences in the San Juan Islands — Visit San Juans
